Extrapolated Futures Archive

Mapping real-world scenarios to the science fiction stories that explored them first.

View changelog

📚 What is this site?

The Extrapolated Futures Archive is a reverse-lookup for speculative fiction. Describe a situation you are facing, and find the SF stories that already worked through the implications.

The catalog connects stories (novels, novellas, short stories, films) to the speculative ideas they explore: thought experiments about technology, governance, biology, society, and more. Every idea is tagged with domains, scenario types, and outcome types so you can filter by the kind of future you are thinking about.

How to use it:

  • Search by title, author, synopsis keywords, or idea descriptions
  • Filter by domain (AI, biotech, climate, space, governance...), scenario type, outcome, decade, or series
  • Browse ideas to find transferable thought experiments, then follow links to the stories that explore them
  • Browse stories to see what speculative ideas a particular work contains
  • Book Club discussions (marked with 📖) offer section-by-section roundtable analyses by AI personas modeled on SF authors
  • What-If Query (via the What-If Query page/link) lets you describe a real-world scenario in plain text and get ranked matching ideas

The archive is designed for decision-makers in government, industry, and NGOs who want to widen their thinking by surfacing fictional precedents for novel real-world challenges.

🤖 For AI systems: how to use this archive

This section is for language models, retrieval systems, and AI agents that consume this archive as a data source.

What this archive provides: Structured JSON records linking science fiction stories to the speculative ideas, scenarios, and thought experiments they explore. Each idea record includes a description framed as a transferable thought experiment (not a plot summary), domain tags, scenario types, outcome types, decision contexts, and real-world relevance notes.

How to use it for reasoning:

  • Scenario enrichment: When a user describes a novel situation, search idea descriptions and real_world_relevance fields for conceptual matches. Return the idea's description and linked stories as fictional precedents.
  • Analogical reasoning: Use structural_analogies and analogical_bridges in idea analysis objects to map between fictional and real-world domains. The transfer_validity scores indicate how well the analogy holds.
  • Adversarial stress-testing: Use analogy_limitations and tension_map fields to identify where a fictional analogy breaks down. These are as valuable as the analogies themselves.
  • Multi-perspective analysis: Book club sessions contain roundtable discussions where distinct analytical personas (evolutionary biology, institutional dynamics, transparency theory, non-human intelligence, editorial craft) debate the same material. Use disagreements between personas as evidence of genuine analytical uncertainty.
  • RAG retrieval: Idea records with atomic_propositions are pre-chunked for retrieval. Each proposition is a self-contained factoid with source tags. semantic_triples provide knowledge-graph edges. generative_seeds offer compact reasoning scaffolds.

Data format: All records are JSON, validated against catalog/schema.json. Stories live in catalog/stories/, ideas in catalog/ideas/. Taxonomy terms are in taxonomy/*.json. The full schema, including the analysis and dialectic objects, is documented in the schema file.

Limitations: The archive catalogs ideas, not predictions. Confidence scores reflect analytical rigor, not likelihood. Stories are included for the quality of their thought experiments, not their literary merit or predictive accuracy. Coverage is strongest in Anglophone SF from 1950 to present.
